title: Gibbs Free Energy & Spontaneity
subtitle: The single number that decides if a reaction will go
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Apply the Gibbs free energy equation őĒG = őĒH ‚ąí TőĒS to predict spontaneity of a reaction at a given temperature
Calculate the temperature at which a reaction switches between spontaneous and non-spontaneous
lessonIntent: Students can calculate őĒG¬į from őĒH¬į and őĒS¬į at a given temperature, predict spontaneity from its sign, and find T at which őĒG¬į = 0.
examSkill: Convert őĒS¬į to kJ before substituting, apply őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į, interpret sign for spontaneity, and find T_cross = őĒH¬į/őĒS¬į.
productionNotes: Mirrors site lesson `subjects/chemistry/year11/module4/lesson13.html`. Worked example: Haber Gibbs (verbatim), őĒH=‚ąí92.4 kJ/mol, őĒS=‚ąí198.9 J/K/mol = ‚ąí0.1989 kJ/K/mol. T=298K: őĒG=‚ąí92.4‚ąí(298√ó‚ąí0.1989) = ‚ąí33.1 kJ/mol (spontaneous). T_cross=‚ąí92.4/‚ąí0.1989=464.6K. T=773K: őĒG=+61.4 kJ/mol (non-spontaneous). Misconception (verbatim): 'negative őĒG means it happens quickly' (it means thermodynamically spontaneous; says NOTHING about rate). Module climax: ties őĒH (L1) + őĒS (L11-12) together.

scenes.caption: Gibbs Free Energy and Spontaneity. Year 11 Chemistry, Module 4.

voiceover.text: Gibbs Free Energy and Spontaneity. Year eleven Chemistry, Module four.

scenes.heading: Will the reaction actually happen?
scenes.body: We've calculated őĒH (the energy change) and őĒS (the disorder change). But neither alone tells you whether a reaction will spontaneously go. That's what Gibbs free energy is for, one number that combines them.

bullets.text: őĒH alone isn't enough, endothermic reactions can be spontaneous.
őĒG = őĒH ‚ąí TőĒS combines them with temperature.
Negative őĒG ‚Üí reaction is spontaneous. That's the test.

scenes.callout: őĒG combines őĒH and őĒS into one go/no-go answer.
scenes.caption: őĒG = őĒH ‚ąí TőĒS, the spontaneity test.

voiceover.text: Here's the climax of Module four. You've calculated delta H ‚ÄĒ the energy change. You've calculated delta S ‚ÄĒ the disorder change. But neither alone tells you whether a reaction will actually go. Think of delta H and delta S like two votes on whether to start a project ‚ÄĒ you need both, weighted by some criterion, to make the final call. But unlike a vote with equal weights, the weighting here is temperature ‚ÄĒ and that's exactly what Gibbs free energy does. Delta G combines them: negative means go.

scenes.heading: őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į
scenes.body: One equation. Three quantities you've already calculated. One critical unit gotcha.

bullets.text: T is absolute temperature, in kelvin (add 273 to ¬įC).
Unit gotcha: őĒS¬į in J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, convert to kJ first.

scenes.callout: Convert őĒS¬į to kJ before substituting (divide by 1000).
scenes.caption: őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į. T in kelvin; őĒS¬į in kJ.

voiceover.text: One equation does it all. Delta G zero equals delta H zero minus T delta S zero. T is absolute temperature, in kelvin ‚ÄĒ so add two-seventy-three to anything given in Celsius. The unit gotcha is critical: delta S zero from last lesson is in joules per kelvin per mole, but delta H is in kilojoules. You must convert delta S to kilojoules first, by dividing by one thousand. Skip that and your answer is off by a factor of a thousand. Then the sign of delta G is your answer: negative means spontaneous, positive means non-spontaneous.

scenes.heading: Haber process: spontaneity and crossover temperature
scenes.question: For N‚āā(g) + 3H‚āā(g) ‚Üí 2NH‚āÉ(g), őĒH¬į = ‚ąí92.4 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ and őĒS¬į = ‚ąí198.9 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. (a) Calculate őĒG¬į at 25 ¬įC. (b) Is the reaction spontaneous at 25 ¬įC? (c) Find the temperature where őĒG¬į = 0.
scenes.coachNote: Convert őĒS to kJ first. Convert T to K. Then substitute carefully, watch the double-negative.
steps: Known: őĒH¬į = ‚ąí92.4 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, őĒS¬į = ‚ąí198.9 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
Find: (a) őĒG¬į at 298 K, (b) spontaneity, (c) crossover T.
Convert: T = 25 + 273 = 298 K; őĒS¬į = ‚ąí0.1989 kJ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
scenes.caption: őĒG¬į(298 K) = ‚ąí33.1 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (spontaneous); T_cross ‚Čą 465 K.

voiceover.text: The Haber process at room temperature. Delta H is minus ninety-two-point-four kilojoules per mole. Delta S is minus one-ninety-eight-point-nine joules per kelvin per mole. First, convert: temperature is twenty-five plus two-seventy-three, two-ninety-eight kelvin. Delta S in kilojoules is minus zero-point-one-nine-eight-nine. Now substitute: delta G equals minus ninety-two-point-four minus two-ninety-eight times minus zero-point-one-nine-eight-nine. Watch the double negative ‚ÄĒ that's minus ninety-two-point-four minus negative fifty-nine-point-two-seven, which is minus ninety-two-point-four plus fifty-nine-point-two-seven, giving minus thirty-three-point-one kilojoules per mole. Negative ‚ÄĒ so spontaneous at room temperature. For the crossover temperature, set delta G to zero: T equals delta H over delta S, both negatives cancel, giving four hundred and sixty-five kelvin ‚ÄĒ about one hundred and ninety-two Celsius. Above that, delta G turns positive.

scenes.heading: "Negative őĒG means the reaction is fast"
scenes.body: Students often think: "őĒG is negative, so the reaction will happen quickly." That's the wrong instinct, and it confuses thermodynamics with kinetics. The correct rule: a negative őĒG means the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous, it CAN occur without external energy. It says nothing about rate. The Haber process is spontaneous at 25 ¬įC but essentially no reaction happens, kinetics, not thermodynamics, limit it.
scenes.callout: Negative őĒG = thermodynamically allowed. Rate is a separate question.
scenes.caption: Negative őĒG means thermodynamically spontaneous, rate is a separate question.

voiceover.text: Here's the trap that catches every student. Delta G is negative, so the reaction will happen quickly. Wrong instinct ‚ÄĒ it confuses thermodynamics with kinetics. The correct rule: a negative delta G means the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous ‚ÄĒ it can occur without an external energy push. It says absolutely nothing about how fast. The Haber process at room temperature is a perfect example: delta G is negative, so it's spontaneous ‚ÄĒ but essentially no reaction happens at twenty-five degrees, because the rate is limited by the strong nitrogen triple bond. Thermodynamics says yes; kinetics says wait.

scenes.heading: Your turn
scenes.question: For C‚āāH‚āĄ(g) + H‚āā(g) ‚Üí C‚āāH‚āÜ(g), őĒH¬į = ‚ąí137 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ and őĒS¬į = ‚ąí120.7 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Calculate őĒG¬į at 25 ¬įC and decide whether the reaction is spontaneous.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: convert őĒS to kJ, T to K. Then őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į.
answerSteps: Convert: T = 298 K; őĒS¬į = ‚ąí0.1207 kJ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
Answer: őĒG¬į = 101 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ released (i.e. ‚ąí101 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) ‚Üí spontaneous at 25 ¬įC.
scenes.caption: őĒG¬į(298 K) = ‚ąí137 + 35.97 = ‚ąí101 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ‚Üí spontaneous.

voiceover.text: Your turn. Hydrogenation of ethene. Delta H is minus one-thirty-seven, delta S is minus one-twenty-point-seven joules per kelvin per mole. Find delta G at twenty-five degrees and decide. Pause and try. T is two-ninety-eight kelvin. Delta S in kilojoules is minus zero-point-one-two-zero-seven. Delta G equals minus one-thirty-seven minus two-ninety-eight times minus zero-point-one-two-zero-seven ‚ÄĒ minus one-thirty-seven minus minus thirty-five-point-nine-seven ‚ÄĒ which is minus one-thirty-seven plus thirty-five-point-nine-seven, giving minus one-hundred-and-one kilojoules per mole. Negative ‚ÄĒ spontaneous at twenty-five degrees.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Always check the sign of őĒG: negative means spontaneous, never fast.
points: őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į, combines enthalpy and entropy at temperature T.
Convert őĒS¬į to kJ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ before substituting (it's the most common error).
Negative őĒG¬į ‚Üí thermodynamically spontaneous; positive ‚Üí non-spontaneous.
Spontaneous ‚Č† fast, rate is a separate (kinetic) question.
scenes.caption: One equation, three quantities, one decision rule.

voiceover.text: Five takeaways for the module climax. One: delta G zero equals delta H zero minus T delta S zero ‚ÄĒ it combines enthalpy and entropy at a given temperature. Two: convert delta S to kilojoules per kelvin per mole before substituting, by dividing by one thousand ‚ÄĒ this is the most common error. Three: T is absolute, in kelvin ‚ÄĒ add two-seventy-three to Celsius. Four: negative delta G means thermodynamically spontaneous; positive means non-spontaneous. Five: spontaneous is not the same as fast ‚ÄĒ rate is a separate, kinetic question. Decision rule: check the sign of delta G ‚ÄĒ negative means spontaneous, never automatically fast.
